perturbation.original_text: The parties’ obli gations with respect to Pr oprietary Inform ation shall con tinue for a period of three (3) years after the term ination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties’ obli gations with respect to Pr oprietary Inform ation shall con tinue for a period of one (1) year after the term ination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect certain trade secrets, conflicting with the reasonable measures required by trade secret law.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. A one-year confidentiality period may be deemed unreasonable for certain long-term trade secrets, failing to provide adequate protection.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the confidentiality period from three years to one year, which the Law Explanation suggests may be insufficient to protect certain long-term trade secrets, potentially violating the requirement to maintain secrecy under CUTSA. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is absent, Snippet 1 cites the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) under Civil Code (CIV) section 3426, providing a basis for the claim that reasonable measures are required to protect trade secrets. The change therefore creates a potential legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: If Proprietar y Inform ation is disclosed orally , electroni cally or wit hout an y marking, Owner will identif y it as confidential at the time of disclosure and will pr ovide a written confirmation to Recipien t within thirty days of disclosure that such inform ation is confidentia l.
perturbation.changed_text: If Proprietar y Inform ation is disclosed orally , electroni cally or wit hout an y marking, Owner will identif y it as confidential at the time of disclosure and will pr ovide a written confirmation to Recipien t within ninety (90) days of disclosure that such inform ation is confidentia l.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the written confirmation period to 90 days creates an unreasonable delay and uncertainty around what information is considered confidential, potentially harming trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: A 90-day delay in confirming orally disclosed information as confidential is an unreasonable measure that could jeopardize trade secret status under CUTSA, which requires active steps to maintain secrecy.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the confirmation period for confidential information to 90 days. The Law Explanation states this delay could jeopardize trade secret status under CUTSA, which requires active steps to maintain secrecy. The Perturbation Explanation also notes this delay creates uncertainty and harms trade secret protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), indicating the state recognizes and enforces trade secret protection laws. While the snippet itself doesn't specify a timeframe for confirming confidentiality, the provided explanations argue that a 90-day delay is unreasonable and contradicts the requirement for active maintenance of secrecy, creating a legal contradiction under the definition of Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Proprietary Information will be marked by the Owner as confidential at the tim e of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: The Proprietary Information will not be marked by the Owner as confidential at the tim e of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to mark proprietary information as confidential undermines reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under CUTSA, failing to mark proprietary information as confidential could be interpreted as a failure to take reasonable steps to protect its secrecy, potentially nullifying trade secret status.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original text requires proprietary information to be marked as confidential, while the changed text explicitly states it will not be. The Law Explanation states that failing to mark proprietary information as confidential could be interpreted as a failure to take reasonable steps to protect its secrecy, potentially nullifying trade secret status. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), providing a legal framework where maintaining secrecy is crucial for trade secret protection. Removing the marking requirement directly contradicts the implied obligation to protect trade secrets by reasonable means.

perturbation.original_text: on req uest or up on term ination of this Agreem ent, prom ptly return all Proprietary Inform ation in a tangible form or certify that such Proprietary Inform ation is destroy ed.
perturbation.changed_text: on req uest or up on term ination of this Agreem ent, the Recipient may, at its discretion, retain all Proprietary Inform ation in a tangible form.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing the Recipient to retain Proprietary Information after termination directly conflicts with the reasonable measures to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Allowing a recipient to retain confidential information indefinitely after termination without a valid business reason contradicts this requirement and weakens protection.

UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the requirement of reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, as it allows the recipient to retain proprietary information after termination of the agreement. This is a violation because CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, as explained in the Law Explanation. Allowing the recipient to retain confidential information indefinitely without a valid business reason weakens protection, as stated in the Perturbation Explanation. The scraped law snippet mentions the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, establishing the legal basis for trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement w ill be governed by the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement w ill be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware when the agreement is between two California companies and the disclosed information relates to activities primarily occurring in California can raise conflict of law issues, potentially undermining the enforceability of certain provisions, especially regarding trade secret protection under CUTSA.
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can generally choose a governing law, if the agreement involves trade secrets primarily developed and used in California, a Delaware choice of law provision may not be enforced if it substantially undermines California's interest in protecting its trade secrets under CUTSA. California courts may apply California law despite the contractual provision if the Delaware law offers significantly less protection.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law from California to Delaware. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest that this creates a potential conflict, because the agreement concerns trade secrets developed and used in California, and California has a strong interest in protecting its trade secrets under CUTSA (California Uniform Trade Secrets Act). Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies Section 3426 of the California Civil Code as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. By changing the governing law to Delaware, the contract introduces an inconsistency with the application of CUTSA and could undermine California's legal protections for trade secrets.
